# Questions in Livestream 227

Live Stream #227: Just One More Question

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:55</a> | How should the differences between the Masoretic Text and the Septuagint version of Esther be explained? | He thinks the Septuagint often took expansive or combined readings, so the longer Greek Esther is not simply proof of corruption. | He says the source texts behind the Septuagint remain partly mysterious, but in books such as Jeremiah and Esther the Greek tradition often appears to preserve longer or combined readings. His explanation is that the translators may have used a comprehensive "kitchen sink" approach, choosing long readings or merging alternatives rather than simply preserving a corrupted form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=929" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:29</a> | Would we know more about Septuagint source texts if the Library of Alexandria had not burned? | Probably not, because the main holdings had already been dispersed long before the later destruction. | He argues that the loss of the Library of Alexandria probably would not have changed much for Septuagint source studies. By the time of the burning, he says, much of the collection had already been moved into satellite libraries, and any relevant sources would more likely have been dispersed and then lost through ordinary decay. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=1048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">17:28</a> | Did the Library of Alexandria burn multiple times? | Yes. He says it burned under Julius Caesar and later during Christian-pagan riots, but by the later burning it was already greatly reduced. | He says the library burned twice, once in Julius Caesar's period and later in the Christian-pagan riots. In his telling, the later destruction affected a greatly reduced institution that had become more like a Neoplatonist school than the great public collection people imagine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=1124" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:44</a> | How should Christians answer slander and escalating political hatred after Charlie Kirk's death? | He says to reject slander, tell the truth, pray, and refuse to become the same hateful thing one condemns. | He treats slander as a serious step in ideological violence because it prepares people to accept worse action against a target. His practical answer is limited: put the truth forward, recognize that not everyone will accept it, pray, and avoid letting anger turn Christians into the sort of hateful people they are opposing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=1210" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">20:10</a> | Wouldn't defamation be an easy win in court? | No. He says defamation cases are expensive, risky, often have low awards, and may cost more than they recover. | He says defamation is rarely an easy remedy because criminal defamation is almost never prosecuted and civil cases are costly. Even a strong case may recover little compared with legal fees, and he adds that judges can still rule against a plaintiff for subjective reasons. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=2052" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:12</a> | Is he related to Peter Falk? | He says he does not know of any relation. | He says that as far as he knows he is not related to Peter Falk, though he leaves it as theoretically possible. The answer is casual: he does not know, probably not, and he has not treated it as something worth tracing. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=2121" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">35:21</a> | What is science, and how should it be understood? | He defines it as a repeatable method of hypothesis, experiment, conclusion, and repetition. | He defines science primarily as a method for understanding the natural world: form a hypothesis, test it by experiment, draw a conclusion, and repeat the test. He distinguishes that from academic work that is theoretical, mathematical, historical, or reconstruction-based but not experimentally repeatable in the strict sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=2415" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:15</a> | Do Christians misunderstand the importance of tough love? | Yes, often by confusing love with license; he says true tough love aims at correction rather than abuse. | He says Christians sometimes misunderstand love as accepting any sin without correction, but New Testament church discipline can require a firm response to public, egregious sin. He also warns that tough love has an abusive extreme; properly used, its purpose is repentance and correction, not cruelty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=2633" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:53</a> | What about natural history? Is it science? | He treats natural history as a reconstruction discipline that grew from natural philosophy, not hard science in the strict sense. | He explains natural history as an outgrowth of natural philosophy, the older category from which modern science developed. Because it reconstructs the prehistoric past with methods closer to historical inquiry, he calls it important but not hard science in the strict experimental sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=2845" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:25</a> | What are his thoughts on censorship in storytelling, especially the Texas anime controversy? | He opposes the law as overly broad, censorious, and likely protectionist. | He says he is aware of the controversy only generally, but understands it as a Texas law affecting adult themes in animation, manga, and comics. He objects that such rules are too broad, restrict storytelling and sales in a censorious way, and may also function to protect local commercial interests. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=3223" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:43</a> | Does he have thoughts on the recent finds from et-Tell? | He has not seen the papers yet and is withholding judgment. | He says he has not yet read the relevant papers or reports, so he cannot give a serious judgment. His answer is explicitly provisional: he is interested, but wants to see the publications before deciding what the finds mean. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=3294" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:54</a> | How should churches deal with petty disputes, gossip, and backstabbing? | He says gossip and slander should be treated as real sins under church discipline, not as harmless behavior. | He argues that gossip, slander, and factional backstabbing are not petty annoyances but sins that damage the church. His answer is that churches should confront them with discipline and repentance rather than tolerate them as ordinary social conflict. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=3651" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:00:51</a> | Did Egyptians ever leave non-serious inscriptions such as dirty jokes or brothel graffiti? | He does not point to brothel inscriptions, but says Egypt did preserve erotic papyri and other non-serious material. | He does not recall evidence specifically like brothel graffiti, but says Egyptians did produce and preserve non-serious or erotic material. He points in general to erotic papyri and similar artifacts rather than treating Egyptian writing as only formal religious or royal material. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=3709" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:01:49</a> | Did Egyptians have political cartoons? | He says no, because political cartoons could have carried a death-penalty risk in that context. | He doubts that Egyptians had political cartoons in the modern sense because public mockery of political power would have been dangerous. His point is not that Egyptians lacked humor, but that criticizing rulers graphically could have been punished severely. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=3940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:05:40</a> | How close are scholars to AI that can accurately read and translate ancient inscriptions? | Still far away, because ancient scripts are too nuanced, sparse, and structurally difficult for current AI. | He says current AI is not close to reliably reading and translating ancient inscriptions. The problem is not just character recognition; ancient writing systems, damaged texts, sparse corpora, and contextual judgment all require expertise that present tools do not supply. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=4472" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:32</a> | Did he ever say the Merneptah Stele's mention of Israel is not really Israel? | No. He says Josh Bowen made that claim, while he thinks the stele does mention Israel or Israelites. | He rejects the idea that he denied Israel in the Merneptah Stele. He says that was Josh Bowen's claim, whereas his own view is that the stele is referring to Israel or Israelites, even if one can debate details of how the name is represented. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=5596" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:33:16</a> | What is his view of God's relation to time, and how does that affect Jeremiah 1:5? | He places God outside time and reads the passage as a statement about foreknowledge rather than God being trapped inside time. | He argues that God is not inside the timeline as creatures are, so divine foreknowledge should not be imagined as God looking ahead from one temporal moment. Applied to Jeremiah 1:5, he takes God's knowing Jeremiah before birth as grounded in God's timeless knowledge, not in God being limited by temporal sequence. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=5765" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:36:05</a> | If God is outside time and space, where is he not, and how should omnipresence be understood? | He rejects a literal everywhere-in-creation view as collapsing into pantheism, while still affirming God's knowledge, power, and ability to be present. | He distinguishes omnipresence from the idea that God is physically spread through creation. He rejects that as tending toward pantheism, while affirming that God knows all things, can act anywhere, and can be present without being spatially contained. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=6258" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:18</a> | Is God with those who suffer terribly at the hands of sin? | Yes. He says God knows, can comfort, and is present even amid horrific evil. | He affirms that God is with those suffering from evil, though not in a way that makes God the author of the evil. His answer emphasizes God's knowledge, compassion, power to comfort, and presence with the sufferer even when the sin itself is horrific. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7013" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:56:53</a> | Did Pilate need Rome's approval to confirm the crucifixion? | No. He says Pilate had the authority to make that decision as prefect. | He says Pilate, as prefect, had the authority to approve crucifixion locally and did not need to send to Rome for confirmation. Rome appointed governors precisely to make provincial judicial and administrative decisions on the empire's behalf. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7292" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:01:32</a> | Was the way Roman governors extracted wealth from provinces considered embezzlement? | Not by Roman standards, as long as Rome received its share; provincial extraction was treated as part of a governor's reward. | He says Roman provincial administration allowed governors to enrich themselves as long as Rome received what it expected. What modern readers might call corruption or embezzlement was often treated as the governor's opportunity to profit from office. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7568" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:08</a> | Why did Jesus eat with tax collectors and sinners, and why were tax collectors viewed so badly? | He says tax collectors were treated as especially bad because they collaborated with Rome and profited from the occupation. | He explains that tax collectors were despised because they worked with the occupying Roman system and could profit by collecting more than required. Jesus eating with them therefore signaled grace toward people considered collaborators and notorious sinners, not approval of their exploitation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:56</a> | What about the incident of Roman standards or flags being placed in the Jewish temple? | He says there were Roman attempts to impose imperial symbols on temple space, and they provoked outrage. | He connects the question to Roman efforts to bring imperial standards or imagery into Jewish sacred space. Because such symbols represented Roman power and could have idolatrous associations, attempts to impose them around the temple produced fierce Jewish resistance. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7771" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:09:31</a> | Does the account of Jonah coincide with the Assyrian crisis around 760 BC under Ashur-Dan III? | He says it can fit that period, which was a low point for Assyria with revolts, plagues, and contraction. | He says the Jonah setting can fit the troubled period around Ashur-Dan III, when Assyria faced revolts, plagues, and loss of control. He treats that as a plausible historical context for Nineveh being receptive, while not claiming the fit proves the narrative. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=7958" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:12:38</a> | Would the solar eclipse of 763 BC have prepared Nineveh to receive Jonah's message? | Not necessarily; he says Assyrians did note eclipses, but whether this prepared them is hard to say. | He acknowledges that Assyrians recorded eclipses and that the 763 BC eclipse belongs to the same broad troubled period. Still, he is cautious about drawing a direct causal line from the eclipse to Nineveh's response to Jonah because the evidence does not let him say that confidently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=8008" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:13:28</a> | Would he look at a video responding to his view of the correct reading of Psalm 22? | No. He says he does not want to give that critic more attention. | He declines to watch or promote the response video because he does not want to reward the critic with more attention. He treats that as a boundary about platforming rather than a concession about Psalm 22. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=8111" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:11</a> | What is the issue with Psalm 22:16? | He says the dispute is whether the text should be read as "pierced" or "like a lion"; he favors "pierced" but is not dogmatic. | He explains that the debate centers on whether Psalm 22:16 should read "pierced" or "like a lion," with manuscript and interpretive arguments involved. He favors the reading that supports "pierced," but frames it cautiously and does not make the entire case depend on absolute certainty. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=8547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:27</a> | Could Ashur-Dan III staying in Assyria during four years of his reign be due to Jonah's preaching? | He doubts it and says the period is too foggy to know what was happening. | He is reluctant to connect Ashur-Dan III's movements directly to Jonah. The period is historically difficult, and while Assyria was unstable, he says the evidence is too unclear to identify Jonah's preaching as the cause of a king's staying in Assyria. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=8596" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:23:16</a> | What are his thoughts on virtue ethics? | He treats it as usable, so long as it is tied to real moral formation instead of empty abstraction. | He sees value in virtue ethics when it concerns the formation of moral character and habits rather than abstract labels. His approval is qualified: it is useful if it directs people toward actual virtue and disciplined moral living. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=8665" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:24:25</a> | Did the saints rise in Matthew 27, or is Licona right that they did not? | He disagrees with Licona and reads the passage as a real, limited resurrection event that points to Christ conquering death. | He disagrees with Michael Licona's nonliteral reading and takes Matthew's report as describing a real resurrection of saints. He understands it as limited and connected to Jesus' death and resurrection, functioning as a sign that Christ has conquered death. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=9421" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:37:01</a> | Why don't documentary hypothesis proponents notice the cognitive load of explaining older-looking details as oral tradition or redaction? | He says theological education often overloads students so they lack time to question what they are taught. | He says students in theological education are often loaded with so much material that they have little time to scrutinize the assumptions behind source-critical models. In that environment, explanations involving oral tradition, redaction, and older-looking details can be absorbed without students weighing the cumulative complexity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=9628" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:40:28</a> | What are his thoughts on the Akkadian terms and Gilgamesh-related question raised in chat? | He says the question is not clear and Akkadian lexicography is outside his specialty, so he cannot answer it well. | He says he is not sure what the question is asking and that Akkadian lexical issues are outside his area of competence. Rather than improvise, he declines to give a strong answer and notes that he would need clearer terms and better expertise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=9725" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:42:05</a> | Was 2 Maccabees 5:4 referring to a comet apparition as a revelation? | He says he is not an Apocrypha specialist and would need to look it up offline. | He does not give a settled interpretation of 2 Maccabees 5:4. He says the Apocrypha is not his specialty and that he would need to look the passage up and study it before judging whether it refers to a comet-like apparition or revelation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=10048" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:47:28</a> | Why do people love fantastic stories of perfect preservation instead of the messier way God works in text criticism? | He says people love myths and simple claims because they avoid the messy details of textual criticism. | He says people are drawn to neat stories of perfect preservation because they are emotionally satisfying and easier than the real complexity of textual transmission. His contrast is that God can work through a messy historical process, but many people prefer a mythic, simplified account. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=10551" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:55:51</a> | Who is Melchizedek? | He identifies Melchizedek as the king of Salem, a Jebusite/Amorite priest-king contemporary with Abraham. | He identifies Melchizedek as a historical priest-king of Salem in Abraham's period, probably connected with the Jebusite or Amorite population of early Jerusalem. He does not treat Melchizedek as a theophany, but as a real royal-priestly figure later used typologically. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=10704" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:24</a> | Have archaeologists found Melchizedek's temple near Jerusalem? | He does not think it has been securely found, though chat notes a debated small temple from roughly the right period. | He does not think archaeologists have securely identified Melchizedek's temple. He acknowledges discussion of a small temple from roughly the relevant period, but keeps the answer cautious because the identification remains debated. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=11218" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:58</a> | Is it common for a standard academic reference work to have real errors? | Yes. He says important works commonly have errors, and the question is whether those errors undermine the useful scholarship. | He says even major academic reference works contain errors, because scholarship is produced by fallible people and often covers enormous amounts of material. The real question is whether the errors are isolated and correctable or whether they undermine the broader value of the work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=11507" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:11:47</a> | Has he ever heard a spirit or demon make a sound? | No. He says the only apparition he has seen was visual and silent, though others report hearing things. | He says he personally has not heard a spirit or demon make a sound. The only apparition he reports seeing was visual and silent, though he acknowledges that other people claim auditory experiences. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=11612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:32</a> | Is there an issue with viewing the ten plagues as polemics against Egyptian gods? | Yes. He says the theory breaks down when specific gods must be matched to each plague, and the text only explicitly says this of Passover. | He says the broad polemic-against-Egyptian-gods theory becomes weak when interpreters try to assign each plague to a specific deity. He notes that Exodus explicitly frames Passover as judgment on Egypt's gods, but does not clearly do that for every plague. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=12064" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:21:04</a> | Do we have evidence that Christians celebrated on Sunday in the first centuries? | Yes. He says early Christians met both with synagogue communities on Saturday and as Christians on Sunday. | He says early Christians had evidence of Sunday observance while also maintaining some Saturday synagogue patterns, especially in Jewish-Christian contexts. In his summary, they could gather with Jews on the Sabbath and also gather specifically as Christians on Sunday, the Lord's Day. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=12305" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:25:05</a> | What does he think about Ecclesiastes? | He likes the book, though he was not familiar with the specific "Ecclesiastes and Blues" reference mentioned in chat. | He says he likes Ecclesiastes, but he is not familiar with the particular "Ecclesiastes and Blues" reference the commenter mentions. His answer is therefore mostly a brief positive reaction to the biblical book, not an analysis of that specific project. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=12524" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:28:44</a> | Why was Samson seen as a judge? | He says judges were liberators meant to rescue Israel from oppression and idolatry, even when they did the job poorly. | He explains that biblical judges are not primarily courtroom figures but deliverers raised up to rescue Israel. Samson counts because he fought Israel's oppressors, even though he was morally compromised and did the liberator role badly. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=12767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:32:47</a> | What is his favorite of the four Gospels? | John, because he is especially drawn to its heavy theology. | He answers John and says its dense theological character is what draws him to it. The preference is about the Gospel's theological depth rather than a claim that the other Gospels are inferior. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=12829" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:33:49</a> | How strong is the "lame origin story" argument for Exodus historicity? | He says it is suggestive of historical grounding, but should not be overstated as proof. | He treats the argument as one cumulative clue: peoples usually do not invent humiliating national origins unless there is some historical grounding. Still, he warns not to overstate it as proof by itself; it is suggestive evidence within a larger case. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=13154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:39:14</a> | Why does Cam seem so interested in the Shroud recently? | He connects it to Catholic-convert culture and says there may also be a platform or audience incentive. | He speculates that the Shroud's appeal in some Catholic-convert spaces may explain the interest, and he also suggests that audience incentives can make it attractive content. He presents this as a likely motivation rather than inside knowledge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=13445" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:44:05</a> | Where did he get the idea of the five stages of ideological violence? | He says he first heard it in a mid-1990s NPR-broadcast lecture, but he can no longer identify the speaker. | He traces the idea to a lecture he heard on NPR in the mid-1990s, but says he no longer remembers who gave it. He is clear that he did not originate the framework and cannot now supply a precise citation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=13796" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:49:56</a> | In which century was the hexagram used as a symbol for the Star of David? | He places it around the late second to early first century BC in the context of ossuary dating. | He places the hexagram's Jewish symbolic use roughly in the late second to early first century BC, based on the dating context he associates with ossuaries. He treats that as an archaeological dating answer, not a full history of later Star of David symbolism. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=13842" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:50:42</a> | Are the United Church and Orthodox Church basically the same? | No. He says they are nothing alike and sharply contrasts the United Church's liberalism with Orthodoxy. | He says the United Church and Orthodox Church are not basically the same and sharply separates their theology and practice. In his answer, the United Church represents liberal Protestantism, while Orthodoxy is liturgical, traditional, and doctrinally very different. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=14212" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:56:52</a> | Can he explain the timeline for the late versus early date of the Old Testament or Exodus chronology? | He contrasts the late and early Exodus timelines and says the "400 years" issue depends on whether the period includes both Egypt and Canaan. | He contrasts a late Exodus chronology with an early one and says the interpretation of the 400 years is central. In his view, the number can be read as including the whole sojourn period in both Canaan and Egypt rather than requiring Israel to spend every one of those years in Egypt alone. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=14522" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:02:02</a> | What is his view of exorcism and the practice of it? | He says exorcism is a church practice that should be done carefully and prayerfully after excluding mental-health causes. | He says exorcism is a real practice of the church but should not be pursued casually. His procedure is cautious: first rule out ordinary and mental-health explanations, then if there is just cause, proceed prayerfully and under proper church care. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=14741" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:05:41</a> | Does he know much about Ed and Lorraine Warren? | Yes, but he does not recommend their work because he considers their methodology unsound and sometimes biased. | He says he knows enough about Ed and Lorraine Warren not to recommend them as reliable guides. His concern is that their methods were not sound and that they sometimes seemed predisposed toward a preferred conclusion. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=14972" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:09:32</a> | Why did Christian tarot cards stop being used that way? | He says tarot was taken over for divination, though a small number still use older decks for Christian teaching. | He says tarot cards may once have had didactic or symbolic Christian uses, but the practice became dominated by divination. Because of that association, Christian use largely disappeared, though he notes a small minority still use older decks for teaching rather than fortune-telling. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15043" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:10:43</a> | What is the worst-case scenario with Ouija boards? | He says the worst case is demonic possession because such tools function as devices of mediumship. | He treats Ouija boards as tools of mediumship rather than harmless games. In the worst case, he says, such practices can open a person to demonic possession or spiritual deception, so he strongly warns against using them. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15396" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:16:36</a> | Is calling for Bloody Mary in a mirror a form of divination? | Yes. He treats it as a bad spiritual practice and says not to play with it. | He says calling for Bloody Mary in a mirror falls under the same kind of forbidden spiritual practice because it attempts contact or manifestation through a ritualized method. His practical answer is simple: do not experiment with it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15559" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:19:19</a> | Do people get interested in the occult because they have too much time on their hands? | No. He says many start flippantly or skeptically, then get pulled deeper when they think there is something to it. | He says occult involvement is usually not just boredom. People may begin as a joke, from curiosity, or even as skeptics, but if they think something responds or works, they can be drawn further into it. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15612" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20:12</a> | What are his thoughts on Catholic and Orthodox prayers to dead saints? | He says they are requests for intercession rather than worship, but he questions their effectiveness compared with praying to God. | He distinguishes prayers to saints from worship, saying Catholics and Orthodox generally understand them as requests for intercession. He still questions the practice because he is not convinced it is effective or necessary when believers can pray directly to God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:25:18</a> | Can people ask God to pass a message to those in Sheol? | He does not think so, saying Abraham's bosom has been relocated and is no longer in proximity to Sheol. | He says he does not think asking God to pass messages to those in Sheol is a proper or workable practice. His explanation is that Abraham's bosom has been relocated and is no longer in the same proximity to Sheol in the way the question assumes. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=15978" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:26:18</a> | Do demons hide themselves from secular people in order to deceive them? | Sometimes. He says spirits use different strategies, including hiding, frightening, or misleading people. | He says demonic deception is strategic and can take different forms depending on the person. Some spirits may hide to keep secular people dismissive, while others frighten, mislead, or present themselves under a different identity. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/am_14bsWNQQ?t=16166" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:29:26</a> | Is it possible to channel good spirits such as God's angels? | No. He says good spirits would not enter in a way that displaces the role of the Holy Spirit. | He rejects the idea that good angels or other good spirits would be channeled through a person. In his view, such displacement of a person by a spirit is not how God's angels work and would confuse or replace the role of the Holy Spirit. |
